moment_text: I was in the shower and I wasn't practicing a self-breast exam because I'm guilty. I didn't know that I was supposed to do that when I was 28 and I was in the shower and I was washing, and I felt the lump. And I said, Ooh. This is strange, and I put my hands back over it again. Um, but inside my head I said, don't worry about it. You're 28. I was just at the gynecologist for a routine appointment two weeks prior, and I had a clinical breast exam at that appointment. So I said to myself, don't, don't stress about this. I'm sure it's just dense tissue. The doctor told you, your, your breasts are dense. And so I kind of just. Even though it stopped me in my tracks, I just pushed it down.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains both barrier signals (lack of information, symptoms normalized) and agency signals (avoidance, self-blame).

moment_text: Um, and I tried to move on. I went about my day and went about the next couple weeks until, um, I got my cycle. And with my period, that lump felt a little bit painful. And so it was always kind of in the back of my mind since I felt it in the shower. But now it was a little bit louder and so I showed it to my. Now husband, um, at the time, oh no, he was my husband at the time. We had just gotten married two months before then. Um, actually, yeah, I'm, I'm the timeline's all mixed up. We'd just gotten married. Um, and I showed it to my husband and I was like, what is this? He's like, oh, I'm sure it's nothing. And then, you know. I would show it to a friend or I would show it to my mom when she came to visit and everyone said to me, I'm sure it's probably nothing but can't hurt to get it checked out.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains both barrier signals (symptoms normalized) and agency signals (seeking support).
notes_for_reviewer: The original paragraph was split here to create two moments of acceptable length, each with a distinct focus (seeking peer support vs. provider encounter).

moment_text: And so I called my gynecologist who I had just seen, and um, finally got in for an appointment and she's like, why are you here? You were just here. Um, and I showed her the lump and she goes, oh, you're, you're so young. Do you have any breast cancer in your family? I said, no. And she said, oh, I'm sure it's nothing but. Still, it didn't sit quite right with me. And so I said, could it hurt to just get some imaging? Like, can we just check it out and make sure, like, I don't feel good about, uh, it's probably nothing like I wanted to know for sure. It was nothing.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains strong barrier signals (provider dismissal) and agency signals (self-advocacy).

moment_text: And so, um, she was hesitant, but she gave me a prescription for a. Ultrasound and told me I wasn't gonna need the biopsy. But here's another prescription for a biopsy just in case. Put it in your purse. Don't show it to the doctor unless they ask for it. And so long story long, um, turns out that, that nothing lump was triple negative breast cancer. And, um. The radiologist, the gynecologist, everybody who was part of this, like unraveling of this diagnosis was just as shocked as I was. They, you know, kept telling me it was nothing 'cause they didn't think it was anything, and it turned out to be. Obviously a pretty aggressive, serious diagnosis and so it was definitely a shock. I had just gotten married, I was really healthy, I was in good shape. I was young. I had no family history of breast cancer, so it just felt like the floor dropped from under me. It was like we were about to go in our honeymoon and start this newlywed life. I was deep in the new stages of like building my career and um, suddenly was being asked to. Make decisions about chemotherapy and surgery and fertility preservation. And, um, yeah, it was, it was a rollercoaster. It was definitely a rollercoaster.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains barrier signals (misdiagnosis, delayed diagnosis) and strong emotional signals within a healthcare system interaction.

moment_text: Um. I, I feel like I'm, I, I feel so lucky and privileged that I was surrounded by like a really strong support system. So my husband, my partner was with me at every appointment. My parents were local, so they were able to come with me to appointments. Um, I was just with my sister-in-law this weekend and she, we were talking about when I was first diagnosed, she gifted me. She just didn't know what to do and wanted to help. So she gifted me this binder to help keep track of my appointments and all the different, um. Uh, medical professionals I was about to meet and honestly, I think I, I blacked out. I just didn't, I couldn't even take it in myself. There was too much, so I leaned on everyone around me to help make decisions and to help navigate it all and, um, until I was able to just like. Find my ground, like feel grounded, find my groove, and be able to move forward. And then from there, it was just so much, it was like this long checklist of everything I had to do in order to reach that finish line of being done right.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains both barrier signals (system navigation difficulty) and agency signals (seeking support, withdrawing).

moment_text: And it's funny 'cause it's this balance of you're leaning on everybody else, but emotionally I didn't want them to know that I was scared. So also just like powering through a check, check, check, let's go through it. And, um. Didn't want them to worry about me any more than they already had to. So just put on a brave face and kept going. But it was definitely, um, in, what's that word? Automatic mode or like, just like, I can't think of the word right now. You know what I mean?
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains barrier signals (fear of judgment), agency signals (silence), and emotional signals within a family system interaction.

moment_text: It's like, I just gotta feel good enough to get this to this appointment and you know, I gotta figure out what I'm gonna wear to, um, lunch with my grandma so she's not worried about me. Right. Like, it's all these other things that you're just. Not facing the big part, which is fear of mortality and, um, what's happening inside your body and all of that. So yeah, it's a lot. It's a lot.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains agency signals (avoidance) and strong emotional signals (fear of mortality) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: I feel like right after being diagnosed, I, I was a mess. I couldn't sleep. I was, I didn't wanna take a shower because that was triggering because that's where I felt my lump. I didn't wanna be alone. Like, I was just so, so scared. And then the adrenaline hit and I was like, all right, we gotta just tackle all these things and get through all these steps. And so. Then I kind of muted all of the emotion and was just like, we're getting through it. We're brave, we're strong. Like let's just check everything off this list. And it really wasn't until I was deemed cancer free, until I finally processed everything. And that's when, um, it just hit me like a ton of bricks. It was like, wow, all of this was really scary and all of this was really hard. And, um. Now that all of the dust has settled and you're on to live your life, like, now's the time to think about it and be scared. And then that's when I just had to lean on other ways to help me deal with that. 'cause yeah, the mental health of survivorship in survivorship is, is, it's tricky. It's hard.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains both barrier signals (lack of support for survivorship mental health) and agency signals (withdrawing, seeking support).
notes_for_reviewer: This moment powerfully describes the delayed emotional processing that occurs after treatment ends, a key theme in survivorship.

moment_text: I, I definitely had to advocate for myself. Just to get a diagnosis, which is interesting. Um, and unfortunately a common experience in the cancer community, in the breast cancer community for young people who go through it. Like, like I mentioned, when I found that lump, the doctor dismissed me. She's like, you're so young, you have no family history. Like, what are you, what are you doing back at this appointment? I just saw you a couple weeks ago. Um, so definitely had to advocate to get that imaging to ultimately get a diagnosis.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains very clear barrier signals (provider dismissal) and agency signals (self-advocacy).

moment_text: But. From there, I feel like because of my age in a lot of ways, um, I don't know, maybe, maybe my team. I feel privileged that my team was open to other things, right? They were like, you have a long, hopefully long life ahead of you, so let's think about fertility preservation. Which again, I know is not, which I know is a privilege, it's not the experience for everybody that they're given that information right when they're diagnosed. And so I was able to go through that before starting chemo and um, most of my medical team. They were young women. So luckily they talked to me a bit about, you know, sexual health and vaginal health and things that I know aren't often talked about in the cancer community, um, between patients and providers.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has emotional signals and system_interaction is not 'none'.
notes_for_reviewer: This is a positive experience moment, highlighting good care, which is why barrier signals are empty.

moment_text: So, yeah, that said, I, you know, there's always advocacy that needs to be done, especially in survivorship, right? When you, you know, because my cancer was triple negative. Survivorship looks a little different there. There's no maintenance medication, there's no scans that are done. So it's really up to you, um, to monitor your own symptoms, which I feel like goes hand in hand with why the mental health part is so hard. But, you know, that takes a lot of advocating. If you feel something, you have to speak up and that's scary. And sometimes, you know, doctors will listen to you and dive right in and help you explore those things. And other times doctors will say, Hey. You don't need to worry about that. Um, even though it feels like the biggest thing in the world to you and you're worrying about it constantly.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has both barrier and agency signals.

moment_text: Um, so advocacy is always part of it. And, um, yeah, I feel like the other piece is just the, um. Secondhand things that you have to deal with as a cancer patient. So insurance and medical leave and all of that. Like, so much advocating needs to be done. And that's another place I really leaned on my support system, um, to help me call my insurance company to help me communicate with, um, my employer and things like that when I wasn't feeling up to it or was too nervous to, or too, you know, didn't have enough of the mental capacity to do it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier, agency, and emotional signals with a system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The system interaction here is multifaceted, including insurance (part of healthcare provider system) and employer (workplace). Workplace was chosen as the primary since it was explicitly mentioned.

moment_text: So. Yeah, unfortunately they give you this diagnosis, tell you all the things that you have to do, and advocacy isn't often on that list, but it's something that you learn is just as important and, um, just as time consuming and hard as all the other things. Yeah, it's almost like something that we. Inadvertently have to do, you know, and, and learn as we go, um, from an advocacy standpoint and just getting that, um, confidence in, in speaking up for yourself is, is just as much a part of the process.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier, agency, and emotional signals with a system interaction.

moment_text: Yeah. I. Definitely leaned on friends and family foremost in the beginning. And I would go to the cancer center and to my appointments and I was the youngest in the room by decades and didn't feel like I could relate to anybody there. And it wasn't till I was, I. Finished with chemo and like approaching my bilateral mastectomy until somebody connected me. You know, my whole treatment, people were connecting me. Oh, my aunt was just diagnosed. You could talk to her. And she was in her sixties. I was in my twenties. So we spoke and it was lovely. But you know, the whole time we didn't really connect on everything. We had some things that we could relate, uh, relate to each other on. But, um, I was thinking about future family and she was thinking about, um. You know, her grandchildren. And so it was just different. And it wasn't until later I was finishing chemo and somebody introduced me to somebody around my age and it was like this first sigh of relief and I was approaching my bilateral mastectomy and my plastic surgeon was showing me pictures of. You know what I could potentially look like after surgery. And they were pictures of women much older than me and that's when I hit a breaking point. I was like, this can't be the only option. Like there has to be other people in their twenties going through this and their early thirties going through this. I can't be the anomaly that like I feel like and people are treating me like, and so I went to social media.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier, agency, and emotional signals with a system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment captures the specific isolation felt due to age difference in a medical setting and among potential support contacts.

moment_text: And I, I wasn't looking for friends. Okay. I wasn't really looking. I didn't want cancer friends, but I was looking for information. I was like, what am I gonna look like? Um, but luckily. While searching, I realized like there's so many other people going through similar things, maybe not the same thing, but maybe somebody, um, navigating a prophylactic mastectomy or somebody with a different type of breast cancer during chemo. And I was like, whoa, there's other people out there. And so I connected with, um, my co-founders at the Breasty, Paige and Bri, eventually Trish and so many other community members, and started to build the beginnings of the Breasty and, you know. I didn't think that's what I needed, but it so desperately was what I needed. And um, yeah, I'm so grateful that that's what unfolded. And it's funny, everyone, I know this isn't really what you asked, but thinking about the beginnings of the recipes, everybody's like, oh, like why did you create it? And there was no intention to become a nonprofit or this like movement in any way, shape, or form. Like we were just creating spaces. Uh, things that we wanted to be at and we were creating, um, resources that we so desperately needed. And so, um, yeah, it was. It was a beautiful unfolding of something that I needed so desperately but didn't know I needed. But my community very quickly transformed into like from family and friends, which were still around me. But you know, when you go into the survivorship stage, you look healthy, you're finished with your appointment, you're finished with active treatment. Um, that support definitely dwindles a bit. So being able to lean on people who truly understood was, uh, definitely a blessing. Like so, so lucky to have the community around me at that point.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier, agency, and emotional signals with a system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment shows the transition from seeking information to unintentionally building a community to fill a support gap.

moment_text: Yeah. Yeah, so it's, we started the breast ease in 2018 and I had just finished radiation. Um, I was still on oral chemo and navigating my reconstruction surgery, so I was still in the, in the middle of all of it. Um, and we started hosting retreats for like 20 to 25 community members at a time. And, you know, I put all this energy I had into like. Setting these retreats up and, you know, leading activities. And it was so cool and I was meeting so many amazing people. And I remember so vividly on like our third retreat, I realized like, wait a second, I don't just need to be leading these, like I could benefit from this. Like, I need this just as much as like the person sitting next to me needs it. And I think that's, that's. One of the beautiful things about the breast seeds, it really is like for the community and led by community members. So, so many of the people, um, that are leading our meetups and our events and speaking on panels are community members themselves, which makes it such a, a beautiful community. But yeah, it was, it was one of our retreats. I just realized like how much work I had to do and how. Many walls I had put up and how fearful I was about my future. And, um, yeah, like I mentioned earlier, just like that stage of survivorship was finally the time where like everything was quiet enough that like finally, like all of these emotions started to be loud enough for me to process. And so, yeah, I realized that I needed community too. It wasn't just something that I could pour into, but it was something I could receive from as well. So.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has agency signals and emotional signals with a system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This is a key realization moment about the reciprocal nature of community support.

moment_text: Yeah. Yeah. So many of my decisions were accelerated after being diagnosed. Um, my partner and I, we had just gotten married and we knew we wanted kids, but we were like. Planning our honeymoon for a few months from then and we're like, oh, we're doing so well in our careers. Let's like continue to grow and travel and do all these things. But then, um, life happens and you have, you're forced to stop and you're forced to make life altering decisions that, um. You don't have a long time to make, you're still in shock, right? And you're forced to make these very quick decisions and think quickly.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: has barrier, agency, and emotional signals with a system interaction.

moment_text: We, we got to that appointment and the doctor started to talk about like eggs versus embryos, and even though we had a lot of trust in each other in our relationship, she also looked at me, the doctor, and was like, you know, cancer is a really hard thing on a marriage, so if you choose embryos, just note.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (making_decisions) and emotional signals (trust) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: You know, the difference between that and the weight of that decision versus eggs. And so I feel very lucky that these things were explained to me, even though there was such urgency to make those decisions. And, um, we were able to, you know, take that information and do what was best for us. And, you know, for the most part.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (time_constraint) and agency signals (making_decisions) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: I think, you know, we made the, the best decisions we could at the time with the information we had, and it ended up working out for us. We have two beautiful children now, healthy children. Um, but some things like, uh, thinking about like surgery and all of that, like I just trusted my doctors blindly. They said, okay, you're gonna have a mastectomy. You're choosing that this is the type of reconstruction that we do here. And I was like, okay. Not knowing that there are.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (lack_of_information) and agency signals (enduring) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: So many other options, other options that are alternative to implants that are different implant types and different, you know, scar positions. Like I didn't know any of that. I just found out what my doctor did and trusted that they knew best and went with that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (lack_of_information) and agency signals (enduring) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: And you know, again. Maybe I would've made different decisions, but for the most part, I made the best decisions that I could have with the information I had at the time. But I think it's really tricky when you're forced to make these, again, life altering decisions, when you're still in shock, when you're scared, when you're really vulnerable.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (time_constraint) and strong emotional signals (fear, overwhelm) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: And, um, again, there's this sense of urgency and pressure, right? Like you're told. Um, you know, your surgery should happen within x amount of time from chemo, or you need to start chemo in X amount of weeks. And, you know, for me, I didn't question that, but now being a community leader, I, I've realized that like you have every right to ask to extend that timeline. You have every right to ask your provider if they. Have another option for you, or if there's another provider that can do the option that you're asking for if they can't.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) and barrier signals (time_constraint) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: And so, um, looking back on little Me, I had, I had no idea that I could question my medical team, you know, I was like, they're the expert. Um, but realizing that, um, as a patient, you're the expert of your own body and you have to live in that body and deal with all of the side effects and, um, ripple effects of the decisions you make.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy) and barrier signals (lack_of_information) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: And so. Yeah, I, I don't know how I would, how I would approach decisions differently, but I think for the most part, um, it's a lot, it's a lot to make those decisions when you're in that mental state and in that timeframe. Right. And it's all new. Um, but shared decision making ultimately is, is something that we all have or are right to. And so I think it's, it's really important. You mentioned that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (time_constraint) and agency signals (self_advocacy) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: Oh yeah. Treatment? Yeah. Okay. Yeah, I get it. Um, yeah, I thought I have this image, like the media shows you somebody bringing the bell and then moving on to their happy life where they live a long fulfilled, like, you know. Live life to the fullest mindset life. And um, it's not that you want it to be that, but it's not that like you, at least for me, when I rang the bell,
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (lack_of_information, stigma) and emotional signals (frustration) within a media system interaction.

moment_text: um, like I said, that was the moment when I was like, Ooh, crap. Like now I don't see my doctor every couple weeks. I see them every three months and then every six months and then once a year and then I graduate to survivorship clinic and I'm on my own. And it's, you know, it's definitely. Um, scary.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong emotional signals (fear, isolation) and barrier signals (fragmented_care) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: It's definitely scary. I think the first few years post active treatment were definitely the hardest for me, just because of that fear of recurrence, um, and being told like the, um, risk of recurrence in those first three years, especially with triple negative. Like I was just scared, like every single sensation. I was worried about, and you know, it impacted my mental health, it impacted my relationships, it impacted, you know, everything. Like, it was all I could think about.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong emotional signals (fear, anxiety, overwhelm) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: And so I definitely needed some tools. I needed the community, I needed therapy, I needed things to help me get through it. Um, and it was different from what I envisioned being done to look like. I thought, oh, my hair's gonna go back. I'm gonna feel good. I'm gonna look good. And, um. Even though I started to look more like my old self and look healthy, I didn't feel good. I was just in this like scared state all the time and like grieving what I could have been doing and like what everything cancer took away from me.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains barrier signals (lack_of_support), agency signals (seeking_support), and emotional signals (grief) within a peer_community system interaction.

moment_text: But fortunately, like as I started to like. Cross another milestone, another year, another month. Like the voice was still there, the fear was still there, but it got a little quieter and I was able to, you know, not be as triggered by things or, you know, if I heard the word cancer in a TV show or a movie, I wasn't like, I, I gotta shut this off. I like can't watch this show and have my day ruined. Like it, it started to just be a little bit more manageable.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional signals (fear, relief) within a media system interaction.

moment_text: But yeah, it's all, um. You know, you get through treatment, it all is such a blur. And then like survivorship, it does move a little slow. Like the grief moves a little bit slowly and it's a little scary. But, um, yeah, there's no finish line is what I'm learning. Like, you know, no matter what, it's always gonna be something that's on my mind. Even now, I'm nine years post-diagnosis and it's still something I think about daily.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (enduring) and emotional signals (grief, fear).

moment_text: For sure daily. Maybe not every hour. Maybe not the first thing in the morning anymore, but it's definitely still something that's part of me and you know. Not just something that you think about, but you get dressed and your scars are present. And you know, I recently underwent a, uh, prophylactic ectomy. So just like all of the things with navigating risk and prevention as somebody impacted by cancer, you know, there's lasting physical effects and lasting emotional and, um. Effects on your mental health.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (making_decisions) and emotional signals (anxiety) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: Yeah. Yeah. I feel, I feel very lucky that in so many ways for the physical effects that I have, because I don't feel like. I feel pretty good. I feel pretty strong, like I feel healthy, um, because my cancer was triple negative. I'm not on any maintenance drugs, so I feel like that's a privilege in some ways. It physically, emotionally, it can be scary that there's nothing just like to protect you, that extra layer of protection. But, um, physically I feel really good and, you know, I. And technically in menopause, but I'm able to go on hormone replacement therapy. So physically I feel pretty good, but I think emotionally, um, you know, there are things that the average person wouldn't think about that I think about often, that I am, I have like bodily sensations that I'm super hyper aware of that like the average person wouldn't even think about.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (tracking_symptoms) and emotional signals (fear, anxiety) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: Like, it sounds so silly, but like, I remember after, um, finishing treatment, like little sensations, like my feet on the floor when brushing my teeth, like. I would feel that, and I never used to feel that or like, you know, my elbow cracking when I reached up. I was like, wait, I never used to notice things like that. But I was so hyper aware of like every sensation, every feeling in my body. And um, I think in some ways because. Finding my own lump. It was like a protection mechanism. It's my body being like, let's stay on high alert because we did it once and it saved our life and we can continue to do that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (tracking_symptoms) and emotional signals (anxiety).

moment_text: And also it was a way to be in charge when your doctor says, like, how do you feel? And that's your symptom management, that's your, um, your protocol for survivorship and managing recurrence. Like, you know, you're hyper aware. And I think just the PTSD of it all rights. Even now things unrelated to breast cancer. Like if I have like a mole that looks funny, I'm like very quick to go to worst case scenario in my mind, or you know, my regular routine appointment with my PCP. Like, I'm more nervous about it probably than the average person just because of all the history.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (tracking_symptoms) and strong emotional signals (fear, anxiety) within a healthcare_provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker explicitly uses the term 'PTSD'.

moment_text: Um, but. Yeah, it's, it's a lot, it's a lot to carry. It's a lot of extra emotional labor that, um, the average person probably doesn't have, but you know, all comes with living. It all comes with living. Which is uh, which is the best side effect.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals (enduring) and emotional signals (overwhelm).

moment_text: I do feel like, at least from like a medical standpoint, like doctor medical teams, they're so good at saving your life and, uh, managing side effects and thinking through, um. All that you need to think about as it relates to your cancer physically. But we're less good and our medical teams are less, less good at helping us, like rebuild our lives post-cancer. And like I'm grateful my hospital has a survivorship clinic, but at the same time it's not exactly what I'm looking for. So, you know, I find myself and a lot of other people are reaching. Outside of the medical space to find additional supports, whether that's with community organ organizations like the breasty or, um, educational resources or, um, just connecting with other people who've been in their shoes on social media or, you know, on different apps and things like that in person.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear barrier signals (fragmented_care, lack_of_support) and agency signals (seeking_support) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: And, you know, one, um. Just like experience I had in this. Period of post-cancer going on to have a family. You know, one thing that I always thought about was like the fear of not being able to breastfeed and like that was a, a really hard thing to go through and, you know, it was talked to me about by my medical team when making decisions, but it. That was kinda it. It was like you won't be able to breastfeed the end, but there were no resources in place. Like nothing. Like here's what you can do instead. Formula is great. Donor milk is an option. Like nobody kind of walks you through those next steps. They tell you maybe what's gonna happen, but they don't walk you through.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong barrier signals (lack_of_information, lack_of_support) and emotional signals (fear) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: But there's so many things like that, right? Like they tell you menopause. Like, here are the things that you're gonna feel and these are the side effects that could happen when you enter medical or surgical menopause. But nobody tells you like, these are other options that you can have that are safe for you. Or like, these are resources and, um, great books you can read, or great providers you can reach out to, um, outside of your oncology team.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear barrier signals (lack_of_information, fragmented_care) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Advocating to your medical team, like you are not there to make friends. Somebody taught me that you're not at your appointments to make friends, right? And of course you can be lovely and respectful, but at the same time, like you need to get your me needs met and you deserve to have your needs met. And so if you don't speak up, who is gonna speak up on your behalf? And, um, it's your body. So you are the expert in your body and you gotta think about your. Um, relationships with your medical team, almost like a partnership, like they're the expert in medicine, but you're the expert of your own body.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) and emotional signals (empowerment) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: You deserve a provider that listens to you. And if it's not happening, you deserve a second opinion. You deserve somebody else, um, to help you reach the decision you need or you want and somebody who's gonna listen. So I think I. Just looking back, I didn't know that second opinions were an option. I didn't think I had time for that and realizing that like, you know, you can make the decision if you have time for that asking. Okay. If I wait another two weeks to, to make this decision, is there any. Con to that, like what are the risks associated with delaying this decision two weeks? Because to your medical team, they might be able to outline that and decide that that's a big deal, but maybe to you it's not a big deal. Maybe that two weeks outweighs, um, the pros of that two weeks outweighs the cons are the risks.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains both strong agency signals (self_advocacy, asking_questions) and barrier signals (lack_of_information) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Yeah, I mean, oh, three things. I think the first thing is just, um, it sounds so cheesy, but it's so true. Like that isolation to finding community, like you think you're the only person going through it. Like you feel like the only one this is happening to. And you know, with media now we're learning more and more too about, um. Uh, breast cancer diagnoses in young people. But, you know, thinking back to myself when I was diagnosed, I was so scared and I thought like I'm the only one in their twenties to get breast cancer. But realizing like there is a community out there, there are so many people, and you can go from that like feeling of isolation to feeling of belonging and like feeling of, um, connected with others is just like such a huge benefit. And like if you have to go through this, like you wanna go through it with people who. It can lift you up. Um, and with that too, like it helps you move from, like when you're diagnosed, there's so much fear, right? You're scared. And not saying that that fear goes away, but it's easier to tackle when you have this like collective, I don't know, not in a toxic positivity kind of way, but this collective resilience. Like there's just this energy to. Even if it's somebody else saying, I'm scared too. Like, that helps you move through it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong emotional signals (isolation, fear, belonging), a barrier signal (social_isolation), and an agency signal (seeking_support) within a peer community interaction.

moment_text: But yeah, just there's so much, um, that community can do for you, whether it's helping you get through, you know, one stage of treatment, helping you celebrate milestones big and small, helping you feel like you belong, helping you learn to advocate for yourself, like, um. Finding ways to like take your power back and share your story. Like opportunities like this are so special because, um, the more people share their stories and the more people, um, you know, have conversations that are really hard, it helps other people and you know, more people can benefit from it. So.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (sharing_story, self_advocacy) and emotional signals (empowerment) within a peer community interaction.
